metric_name: Revenue Pull vs. Cost Push — framework definition
metric_value: Revenue Pull: manage business for smooth order/shipment flow even at cost of market share; emphasis on cost control. Cost Push: ramp product development, marketing, and capacity ahead of hard orders; emphasis on market share. Most fast-track firms operate in Cost Push mode.
notes: Casale's most significant analytical contribution in the EVP series; this framework is the conceptual anchor for evaluating hypergrowth P&L quality.

metric_name: FTI FY82 Plan — annual revenue target
metric_value: $460 million
notes: FTI FY82 plan: Q1 $100M, Q2 $110M, Q3 $120M, Q4 $130M. Sequential growth of ~10% per quarter baked into plan.

metric_name: FTI FY82 Plan — COGS as % of revenue
notes: COGS planned flat at 40% all four quarters; gross margin 60%.

metric_name: FTI FY82 Plan — RD&E as % of revenue
notes: R&D and Engineering planned flat at 10% all four quarters.

metric_name: FTI FY82 Plan — SG&A as % of revenue
notes: Selling General & Administrative planned flat at 30% all four quarters.

metric_name: FTI FY82 Plan — pretax income as % of revenue
notes: 20% pretax margin baked into plan; net margin 12%; tax rate 40%.

metric_name: FTI FY82 Plan — annual EPS target
metric_value: $2.75 per share
notes: Q1 $0.60, Q2 $0.66, Q3 $0.71, Q4 $0.77. Avg shares ~20.1M. P/E multiple: 40x at time of plan.

metric_name: FTI P/E multiple
metric_value: 40x earnings
notes: GOH! references 'robust 40 P/E multiple' as the earnings-expectation anchor. Casale implies this multiple creates fragility — any EPS miss produces disproportionate stock impact.

metric_name: FTI Q2 cost-push problem — advertising expense surprise
metric_value: $2 million of advertising expenses lost in rudimentary control system; had been pushed from Q1 into Q2. Impact: 6 cents EPS at $110M revenue — equivalent to Q1 earnings.
notes: The $2M advertising oversight illustrates how expense-control failures in cost-push mode create EPS surprises even when revenue is on plan.

metric_name: FTI Q2 Actual vs. Plan — revenue beat used to cover expense miss
metric_value: Revenue: $112M actual vs. $110M plan (+$2M); SG&A: $35M actual vs. $33M plan (+$2M); Net income: $13.2M (flat); EPS: $0.66 (on target)
notes: FTI used $2M revenue beat to absorb $2M SG&A surprise; EPS unchanged but gross margin and SG&A% both shifted. This is the 'solution' that masks the underlying control system failure.

metric_name: FTI shipping date manipulation — 'stopped clock' practice
metric_value: Factory management planned push on February 29 1982 (non-leap year); shipments went out during wee hours of February 30; shipping dock date stamp stuck on February 28.
notes: Casale describes this as mildly comical but notes it is technically a GAAP violation; auditors would only detect it through 'exhaustive investigation.' Cross-reference Tandem and Datapoint real-world analogues.

metric_name: FTI FY82 Actual — full-year revenue (cost-push outcome)
metric_value: $478 million vs. $460M plan (+3.9%)
notes: FTI beat full-year revenue plan by borrowing from future periods and using questionable practices in Q3; Q4 federal agency budget flush and auto maker modernization order provided cover.

metric_name: FTI FY82 Actual — full-year EPS (cost-push outcome)
metric_value: $2.75 per share (matching original plan to the penny)
notes: EPS matched plan exactly despite revenue overage because cost-push drove incremental SG&A above plan; net margin contracted from planned 12.0% to actual 11.5%.

metric_name: FTI FY82 Actual — SG&A as % of revenue
metric_value: 31.2% actual vs. 30.0% planned (+1.2 percentage points)
notes: SG&A erosion is the key diagnostic signal in the cost-push P&L; analysts could observe this but could not distinguish between legitimate spend and control failures.

metric_name: FTI FY82 Actual — pretax margin
metric_value: 19.0% actual vs. 20.0% planned (-1.0 percentage point)
notes: Margin erosion visible but modest; individual quarter variation (Q2: 19.5%, Q3: 18.2%, Q4: 18.8%) creates noise that masks the systematic cost-push problem.

metric_name: Incremental sales required to cover incremental cost-push expenses
metric_value: Nearly $20 million of incremental sales required just to cover the incremental expenses running ahead of plan.
notes: Key quantitative insight: high gross margins make it 'far easier' to cover incremental expenses via revenue versus a competitor with poor gross margins — so FTI gains market share but at the cost of earnings quality.

metric_name: FTI tax rate variation — quarterly vs. annual
metric_value: Annual rate: 39.3%; quarterly excursion range: 39.2% to 41.1% (1.4 percentage point range); CFO uses annual rate adjusted quarterly for over/under estimation.
notes: Casale notes that tax rate variation is a legitimate CFO tool and within accepted norms at 1.4 percentage points; analysts cannot distinguish tax management from tax manipulation.

metric_name: GAAP limitation — quarterly interim statements
metric_value: Quarterly financial statements are technically 'interim statements': neither audited nor carry same weight as annual statements. Many annual expenses (benefits, annual report costs) occur in peaks but are spread over quarters per matching principle.
notes: Critical investor note from Casale: quarterly statements have lower audit scrutiny; auditors are not paid to detect earnings smoothing absent serious discrepancy in quarterly review.

metric_name: GAAP-violation escalation ladder — cost-push dilemma responses
metric_value: Stage 1 (legitimate): Sales promotions, discounting, compensation adders. Stage 2 (legitimate): Borrowing sales from future periods via backlog. Stage 3 (legitimate): New product introductions, customer incentives, extended payment terms. Stage 4 (GAAP-violating): Cover the clock at quarter end. Stage 5: Book shipments where paperwork is questionable. Stage 6: Take contingency sales into revenue. Stage 7: Book as sales items not actually sold using intermediaries.
notes: This escalation ladder is Casale's most prescient analytical contribution: it describes precisely the channel-stuffing and revenue-recognition fraud patterns that appeared in 1990s tech companies (Sunbeam, Lucent, MicroStrategy, etc.).

metric_name: Prediction: Cost-push companies will face recurring control system failures and escalating GAAP-violation temptations
metric_value: FTI's rudimentary control system is identified as a structural weakness; CFO warns another $1-2M oversight could pop up at any time; MBA defers control system upgrade to FY84.
notes: Casale predicts that deferring control system improvements while in cost-push mode creates compounding risk; the prediction was borne out across the 1983-84 tech sector correction.

metric_name: Prediction: Cost-push companies will face recurring control system failures and escalating GAAP-violation temptations
metric_value: [UNVERIFIED]

metric_name: Auditor limitation — quarterly review scope
metric_value: Auditors (CPA firm) are not paid to tell management how to smooth earnings; quarterly review less stringent than annual audit; stopped-clock shipment timing would require exhaustive investigation to detect.
notes: Casale exposes the structural audit gap in quarterly reporting that creates the detection window for GAAP violations; this gap would not be formally addressed until Sarbanes-Oxley (2002).

metric_name: FTI Q3 sales paperwork quality issue
metric_value: Auditors noted Q3 sales paperwork was not as tidy as liked; by end Q4, problem mostly resolved; question of revenue restatement was a close call; CPA recommended better controls to Audit Committee.
notes: FTI's near-miss on restatement closely mirrors the real Tandem situation; Casale is using FTI to make the Tandem/Datapoint accounting issues comprehensible to institutional investors.

metric_name: Mutual admiration trap — FY83 pressure amplification
metric_value: After a banner year, investors and management mutually reinforce each other; explicit urgings to make FY83 even better; the cycle resets at a higher baseline of expectation.
notes: Casale identifies the ratchet effect: each successful year resets the expectation baseline upward, making the next year's cost-push problem more severe.

metric_name: Balance sheet effects — preview
metric_value: Balance sheet will provide additional evidence of cost-push severity, but some exaggerated signals; to be analyzed in next edition.
notes: EVP-4 ends with forward reference to balance sheet analysis (EVP-5 or subsequent); cash, receivables, and inventory effects of cost-push operations are the logical next analytical layer.
